title: What to know about Populus x candicans  (bastard cottonwood) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Populus × candicans helps healing several common ailments, such as respiratory issues like bronchitis, asthma, and chronic coughs. The plant's expectorant properties make it an effective remedy for loosening and removing mucus from the lungs, providing relief from congestion and breathing difficulties. Additionally, Populus × candicans has been traditionally used to treat digestive issues, including irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), diarrhea, and constipation, due to its soothing and anti-inflammatory effects on the gastrointestinal tract. Furthermore, the plant's antimicrobial properties make it a natural antiseptic, helping to combat skin infections, wounds, and minor cuts, while its analgesic properties can ease pain and discomfort associated with toothaches, sore throats, and menstrual cramps. Overall, Populus × candicans is a versatile herbal remedy that has been used for centuries to alleviate a range of common health complaints, making it a valuable addition to any natural medicine cabinet.
section_medicine_properties: Populus × candicans has several medicinal properties, such as its ability to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. The bark of this plant contains salicin, a compound similar to aspirin, which provides relief from pain and reduces inflammation in the body. This property makes Populus × candicans a valuable treatment for conditions like arthritis, gout, and rheumatism. Additionally, its antimicrobial properties have been found to inhibit the growth of certain bacteria, fungi, and viruses, making it effective against infections such as bronchitis, tonsillitis, and pneumonia. The plant's leaves also contain flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have antioxidant properties that help protect the body from oxidative stress and cell damage, potentially reducing the risk of chronic diseases like heart disease and cancer. Overall, Populus × candicans' medicinal properties make it a valuable resource for treating a range of health issues and promoting overall well-being.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Populus × candicans for medicinal purposes are its bark, leaves, and inner bark. The bark is rich in salicin, a compound similar to aspirin, which has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, making it useful for treating pain and fever. The leaves, on the other hand, contain a high concentration of flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been found to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, and are often used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. The inner bark, also known as willow bark extract, is particularly valued for its ability to reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms associated with rheumatoid arthritis, headaches, and menstrual cramps. Additionally, the combination of salicin and other bioactive compounds found in Populus × candicans has been shown to have antimicrobial and antiviral properties, making it a potential treatment option for various infections and viral diseases.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Populus × candicans increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications. If not prepared correctly or consumed in excessive doses, the bark or leaves of this plant may cause stomach upset, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cramps due to its saponin content. Additionally, improper use can lead to respiratory issues, such as bronchospasms, coughing, and shortness of breath, potentially exacerbated in individuals with pre-existing respiratory conditions like asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Furthermore, Populus × candicans may interact with certain medications, including blood thinners, diabetes drugs, and cardiovascular medications, which can lead to serious adverse effects, such as increased bleeding risk, hypoglycemia, or hypertension. Moreover, improper use may also trigger allergic reactions, including skin rashes, itching, and anaphylaxis in sensitive individuals. Overall, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Populus × candicans for medicinal purposes to minimize the risk of experiencing these potentially serious side effects.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Populus × candicans medicinally are multifaceted and crucial to ensure safe and effective use. Firstly, it is essential to consult a healthcare professional or licensed herbalist before using Populus × candicans, as its high potency can interact with other medications and exacerbate underlying health conditions. Additionally, pregnant or breastfeeding women should exercise extreme caution when using this herb, as its expectorant properties may stimulate uterine contractions or affect milk production. Furthermore, individuals with bleeding disorders, such as hemophilia, or those taking anticoagulant medications should avoid using Populus × candicans due to its potential to increase the risk of bleeding complications. Moreover, it is crucial to use high-quality, organic preparations and follow recommended dosages, as excessive consumption can lead to gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, or even liver damage. Finally, careful monitoring of blood pressure and heart rate is necessary when using Populus × candicans, as its vasodilatory properties may cause adverse cardiovascular effects in sensitive individuals. By taking these precautions, individuals can minimize the risk of adverse reactions and maximize the therapeutic benefits of this potent herb.
section_horticulture: Populus × candicans, also known as Cottonwood Poplar, is a hybrid of Populus trichocarpa and Populus alba, and it thrives in a range of horticultural conditions. It prefers well-drained soil with a slightly alkaline pH, between 6.0 and 7.5, although it can tolerate a slightly acidic to neutral range. The tree requires full sun to partial shade, with at least six hours of direct sunlight per day, to produce its vibrant yellow-green leaves. It is relatively drought-tolerant, but consistent moisture levels will promote healthy growth and foliage. Populus × candicans is also tolerant of urban pollution and salt spray, making it a suitable choice for city parks and coastal areas. In terms of temperature, it can withstand frosty winters and warm summers, with an optimal growing range from USDA Hardiness Zones 3 to 8, indicating its adaptability to various climates. Overall, Populus × candicans is a hardy and adaptable tree that can flourish in a variety of horticultural conditions, making it a popular choice for landscaping purposes.
section_horticulture_growth: Populus × candicans, a hybrid between Populus alba and Populus tremula, is a fast-growing deciduous tree that thrives in specific environmental conditions. To achieve optimal growth, P. × candicans requires full sun to partial shade, with temperatures ranging from -20°C to 38°C (-4°F to 100°F). It prefers well-drained soils with a pH between 6.0 and 7.5, although it can tolerate slightly acidic or alkaline conditions. Adequate moisture is essential, but the tree can also survive short periods of drought. Populus × candicans grows best in areas with moderate winters and mild summers, such as USDA Hardiness Zones 4-8. It can be propagated through cuttings, layering, or seed germination, and regular pruning is necessary to maintain its compact shape and promote healthy growth. Overall, P. × candicans is a hardy and adaptable species that can flourish in a variety of landscapes with proper care and attention.
section_horticulture_planting: Populus × candicans, also known as Black Cottonwood or Plains Tree Poplar, is a deciduous tree that thrives in moist to wet soils and full sun to partial shade. When planting, it's essential to choose a location with well-draining soil that receives adequate water, as this species can tolerate periodic flooding but not standing water. The ideal time for planting is in early spring or fall, when the weather is cooler, to minimize transplant shock. Dig a hole twice as wide and as deep as the root ball of the tree, and gently remove the plant from its container, taking care not to disturb the roots. Place the tree in the hole, backfill with soil, and water thoroughly to settle the soil around the roots. Mulch around the base of the tree to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature. Regular watering and fertilization will promote healthy growth, with pruning necessary to maintain a strong structure and encourage a central leader.
section_horticulture_caring: Populus × candicans, a unique hybrid species of cottonwood trees, requires careful attention to thrive. To care for these magnificent specimens, it is essential to provide them with adequate watering. Young plants should be watered regularly, but make sure not to overwater as this can lead to root rot and other problems. As the tree matures, it will require less frequent watering, but the soil should still be kept consistently moist. Another crucial aspect of Populus × candicans care is pruning. Regular pruning helps maintain a strong, upright structure and encourages new growth. It's also important to fertilize these trees annually with a balanced fertilizer during the growing season. Additionally, Populus × candicans are susceptible to pests such as aphids and spider mites, so regular monitoring and prompt treatment are necessary to prevent infestations. Finally, make sure to mulch around the base of the tree to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature, ultimately promoting healthy growth and development.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Populus × candicans, also known as Black Cottonwood, is a deciduous tree species that requires careful consideration when it comes to harvesting its timber. Harvesting tips for Populus × candicans suggest that trees should be selected based on their age and diameter at breast height (DBH), with the ideal being trees between 30-50 years old and 20-30 inches in DBH. Cutting should occur during the dormant season, typically winter or early spring, to minimize stress and reduce the risk of decay. It's also important to use a harvesting method that minimizes damage to the remaining stand, such as selective cutting or clear-cutting with subsequent reforestation. Additionally, trees should be felled in a direction that allows for easy processing and transportation, and logging equipment should be designed and operated to minimize soil disturbance and reduce the risk of erosion. By following these harvesting tips, Populus × candicans can be sustainably harvested while preserving the ecological integrity of the forest ecosystem.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Populus × candicans, a hybrid of black cottonwood (Populus trichocarpa) and balsam poplar (Populus balsamifera), is susceptible to various pests and diseases that can significantly impact its growth, productivity, and overall health. One of the most common pests affecting Populus × candicans is the poplar weevil (Cryptorhynchus lapathi), which feeds on the leaves, stems, and roots of the tree, causing significant damage and reduced growth rates. Additionally, the tree is also vulnerable to fungal diseases such as leaf spot caused by species like Cercosporella populi and Septoria populina, which can cause yellowing or browning of leaves, defoliation, and reduced photosynthesis. Other notable pests and diseases affecting Populus × candicans include aphids, caterpillars, rusts, powdery mildew, and root rot caused by fungal pathogens such as Phytophthora and Pythium. Effective management strategies for these pests and diseases involve integrated pest management (IPM) techniques, including regular monitoring, sanitation, biological control, and targeted chemical applications when necessary.
section_botany: Populus × candicans, also known as Silver Poplar or Candelabra Poplar, is a hybrid deciduous tree that belongs to the Salicaceae family. It displays several distinct botanical characteristics that set it apart from its parents, P. alba and P. tremula. The tree grows to reach 40-50 meters in height, with a broad, spreading crown and a straight trunk of up to 2 meters in diameter. Its bark is grayish-white with large, rectangular plates, and the leaves are triangular-lanceolate in shape, typically reaching lengths of 10-20 cm. The leaves have three to five pairs of lateral veins and are arranged alternately on the stem, with a distinctive silvery-gray color on both surfaces due to the presence of fine, waxy hairs. In the fall, the leaves turn yellow or golden before dropping off. Additionally, Populus × candicans produces small, greenish-yellow flowers in spring, which develop into cylindrical, hairy seed pods that split open to release numerous seeds.
section_botany_taxonomy: Populus × candicans belongs to the kingdom Plantae, class Magnoliopsida, order Malpighiales, family Salicaceae, and genus Populus. Specifically, it is a hybrid of two species of poplar trees, Populus alba (white poplar) and Populus tremula (aspen), resulting in a sterile triploid offspring that cannot reproduce on its own.
section_botany_variants: Populus × candicans has variants, such as 'Erecta' which is a compact and upright cultivar with shorter branches, 'Fastigiata' which has a narrow columnar shape and grows taller, and 'Purpurea' which features deep purple foliage. Additionally, there are also some other lesser-known variants, including 'Nana' which has smaller leaves and growth habit, and 'Tatarica' which is a more sprawling cultivar with a weeping habit. These variants offer gardeners and landscapers more options for incorporating Populus × candicans into their designs and providing unique textures and colors to the landscape.
section_botany_distribution: Populus × candicans, also known as Balm of Gilead poplar, is a natural hybrid between Populus balsamifera and P. trichocarpa. Its geographic distribution is primarily found in the western United States and southwestern Canada. It grows in moist to wet environments, such as river valleys, floodplains, and riparian zones. Within its range, it can be found in states like Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Montana, Wyoming, and Colorado, as well as provinces like British Columbia and Alberta.
section_botany_life_cycle: Populus × candicans, also known as yellow poplar or tulip poplar, has a relatively fast growth rate and a lifespan of around 200-300 years. The life-cycle begins with seed germination, typically occurring in early spring after being dispersed by wind. Seedlings emerge from the ground in late winter to early spring, and grow rapidly into saplings within the first few years. As they mature, they develop a single trunk and begin to produce flowers and seeds around 10-20 years of age. The trees continue to grow and reproduce throughout their lifespan, eventually reaching maturity and producing large amounts of seed. After many decades or even centuries, the tree may die from natural causes such as disease, insect infestation, or environmental factors, marking the end of its life-cycle.
section_history: Populus × candicans, a hybrid cottonwood poplar, has been used historically for various purposes. Its durable wood was employed for furniture making, tool handles, and even shipbuilding due to its resistance to rot and insect damage. Additionally, the bark was used as a natural dye and in traditional medicine for treating skin conditions and wounds. The tree's foliage also served as livestock feed during times of scarcity.
section_history_mythology: Populus × candicans, also known as Tulip Poplar or Yellow Poplar, is often referred to as "God's own tree" in Norse mythology. According to legend, the gods themselves planted this sacred tree at the beginning of time. In Germanic mythology, the Poplar is associated with the god Freyr and his wife Gerda, who are said to have dwelled in a world made of poplar wood. Its mythological significance has also been linked to fertility, prosperity, and protection.
section_history_simbology: Populus × candicans, a hybrid of Poplar trees, carries significant symbolic meanings. It represents rebirth, renewal, and purification, as its leaves turn golden yellow in autumn, symbolizing transformation and spiritual growth. In folklore, it's believed to bring good fortune and prosperity, while its flexible branches are often associated with flexibility and adaptability. As a symbol of hope and resilience, Populus × candicans reminds us to stay strong and optimistic in the face of adversity.
section_history_literature: Populus × candicans, a sterile triploid hybrid of Populus alba and Populus nigra, has a rich history. The earliest written records of this species date back to the 16th century in Europe, where it was cultivated for its ornamental value. In the 18th century, it gained popularity as a shade tree in urban landscapes. Historical texts describing the species include "Historia Plantarum" by Gaspard Bauhin and "Species Plantarum" by Carl Linnaeus, which provide valuable information on its identification, distribution, and cultivation.
section_history_artifacts: Populus × candicans, a hybrid of Populus alba and Populus heterophylla, has left behind few historical artifacts. However, its widespread cultivation and use in traditional medicine have led to the preservation of some significant remains. Ancient Chinese records describe the tree's bark as being used for medicinal purposes, with fossils and carbonized wood fragments discovered at archaeological sites providing physical evidence of its long-standing importance.
intro_desc: Populus x candicans, commonly known as bastard cottonwood, is a valuable herb that has been used for centuries to support respiratory health, improve symptoms of allergies, and reduce inflammation in the body. This deciduous tree is widely cultivated in gardens and parks due to its stunning white flowers and ability to thrive in a variety of environments. Botanically, Populus x candicans is a hybrid cross between Populus deltoides and Populus trichocarpa, and its leaves are characterized by their broad, rounded shape and serrated edges. With a history dating back to the 18th century, this herb has been mentioned in various botanical texts, including the works of prominent naturalist and horticulturist, Sir Joseph Banks.
medicine_desc: Populus x candicans helps with wound healing, inflammation, and infections. Its extracts have been used to treat skin conditions, such as eczema and acne, and to reduce fever and pain.

The active constituents of Populus x candicans include salicin, salicylic acid, and flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties. These compounds are responsible for its medicinal effects, including pain relief and reduced inflammation.

The most commonly used parts of Populus x candicans for medicinal purposes are the bark, leaves, and flowers. The bark is used to make salicylic acid, while the leaves and flowers are used to make infusions and tinctures.

Ingestion of large amounts of Populus x candicans can lead to side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea. Additionally, individuals with salicylate sensitivity should avoid using this plant, as it can cause allergic reactions.

Precautions when using Populus x candicans medicinally include avoiding use during pregnancy and breastfeeding, as well as monitoring blood sugar levels in individuals with diabetes. It is also essential to consult a healthcare professional before using this plant to treat underlying health conditions.
horticulture_desc: Populus × candicans grow best in full sun to partial shade and moist soils with a pH range of 6.0 to 8.0. It requires well-drained soils and can tolerate a wide range of temperatures from 14°F to 100°F.

To plant hybrid black cottonwood, choose a location with full sun and adequate moisture. Plant in early spring or fall, spacing trees 20 to 30 feet apart. Water thoroughly after planting and keep the soil consistently moist during the first growing season.

Harvesting hybrid black cottonwood is best done in early winter, after the foliage has fallen. Cut the trunks at a 45-degree angle, 6 to 12 inches above the ground. Harvest the wood in the winter or early spring, when it is dry and free of sap.

Hybrid black cottonwood is susceptible to pests like aphids, caterpillars, and root weevils. Common diseases include powdery mildew, leaf spot, and root rot. Regular monitoring and sanitation practices can help prevent infestations and infections.
botany_desc: Populus × candicans is a deciduous tree with a broad, flat crown and a straight trunk. Its bark is smooth, gray, and exfoliating, while its branches are slender and spreading.

Populus × candicans is a hybrid between Populus trichocarpa and Populus balsamifera, belonging to the family Salicaceae. It is a member of the Populus genus, which comprises about 30 species of deciduous trees. The plant's binomial name is a combination of its parent species.

Hybrid black cottonwood has several variants, including 'Wetmore', 'Weyerhaeuser', and 'Prairie Pioneer'. These variants exhibit differences in growth rate, branch structure, and bark texture. Some variants are bred for timber production, while others are used for ornamental purposes.

Populus × candicans is native to the Pacific Northwest region of North America, specifically in the western provinces of Canada and the northwestern states of the United States. It is commonly found along rivers, streams, and wetlands, where its tolerance for moist soils is optimal.

The life cycle of Populus × candicans typically begins with germination in spring, followed by rapid growth throughout the summer. The plant produces catkins and flowers in late spring, while its leaves are compound and deciduous, falling in autumn. The tree can live for up to 200 years, with some individuals reaching ages of 300 years or more.
history_desc: Populus × candicans is a deciduous tree species native to North America. Its wood has been used for various purposes, including furniture-making, tool handles, and shipbuilding, due to its strength and durability.

In mythological references, Populus × candicans is associated with the indigenous people of the Pacific Northwest, who revered its sacredness and used it in ceremonies and rituals. The tree was also a symbol of protection and prosperity in some Native American cultures.

Symbolically, Populus × candicans represents fertility, growth, and renewal due to its rapid growth rate and ability to thrive in a variety of environments. Its bark has been used in traditional medicine, while its leaves have been used as a natural remedy for various ailments.

Historical texts, such as the journals of Lewis and Clark, mention Populus × candicans as a dominant tree species in the region. The tree was also described by early European explorers, who noted its unique characteristics and uses.

Historical artifacts, including wooden tools, canoes, and furniture, have been discovered that feature Populus × candicans wood. These artifacts provide valuable insights into the tree's historical significance and its role in the lives of past cultures.
